title: LAG / LEAD for Period-over-Period Comparison
when_to_use: comparing current period to previous period

dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: LAG returns NULL for the first row — division by LAG(...) will return NULL not error
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use NULLIF to guard: (current - LAG(current)) / NULLIF(LAG(current), 0) for growth rate

mistake_patterns.mistake: Forgetting ORDER BY in OVER() clause
mistake_patterns.symptom: LAG returns wrong values or error 'window function requires ORDER BY'
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT amount, LAG(amount) OVER () AS prev FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT amount, order_date, LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_amount FROM orders

mistake_patterns.mistake: Mixing aggregation and window functions without subquery
mistake_patterns.symptom: ERROR: aggregate functions are not allowed in window function arguments
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT SUM(amount), LAG(SUM(amount)) OVER (ORDER BY month) FROM orders GROUP BY month
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT month, revenue, LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_revenue FROM (SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS month, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM orders GROUP BY 1) t

concept_explanation.what_it_does: LAG(col, n) returns the value of col from n rows before the current row in the defined window. LEAD looks forward. Essential for period-over-period comparisons without self-joins.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Think of a spreadsheet where you reference the cell one row above — LAG(col) is that reference, with ORDER BY defining what 'above' means.

template: SELECT period, metric, LAG(metric) OVER (ORDER BY period) AS prev_metric, (metric - LAG(metric) OVER (ORDER BY period)) / NULLIF(LAG(metric) OVER (ORDER BY period), 0) AS growth_rate FROM (SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', date_col) AS period, SUM(value_col) AS metric FROM your_table GROUP BY 1) t ORDER BY period
anti_patterns: Never use LAG without ORDER BY in OVER()
Avoid LAG across PARTITION boundaries without PARTITION BY

title: Running Totals and Cumulative Sums

dialect_traps.construct: ROWS vs RANGE in window frame
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Default frame is RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW — safe for running totals but slow on large tables
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for deterministic performance

mistake_patterns.mistake: Using SUM without window frame — gets total not running total
mistake_patterns.symptom: All rows return the same grand total value
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT order_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER () AS running_total FROM orders
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT order_date, amount, SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total FROM orders

concept_explanation.what_it_does: SUM() OVER (ORDER BY ...) computes a cumulative sum up to and including the current row, ordered by the specified column.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Each row's running total = its own value + the running total of all previous rows.

template: SELECT period, revenue, SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY period ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS cumulative_revenue FROM (SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS period, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM orders GROUP BY 1) t
anti_patterns: Do not use running totals across multiple partitions without PARTITION BY

title: RANK / DENSE_RANK / ROW_NUMBER for Top-N Queries

dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: RANK skips numbers after ties (1,1,3). DENSE_RANK does not (1,1,2). ROW_NUMBER always unique.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use ROW_NUMBER for deduplication. Use DENSE_RANK when you want contiguous ranks despite ties.

mistake_patterns.mistake: Filtering on window function result in WHERE instead of subquery
mistake_patterns.symptom: ERROR: window functions are not allowed in WHERE
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS revenue, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(amount) DESC) AS rnk FROM orders WHERE rnk <= 10 GROUP BY 1
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT customer_id, revenue FROM (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS revenue, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(amount) DESC) AS rnk FROM orders GROUP BY 1) t WHERE rnk <= 10

concept_explanation.what_it_does: RANK/DENSE_RANK/ROW_NUMBER assign ordinal position within a window. Filtering on rank in a subquery is the standard pattern for top-N queries.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Think of it as adding a position column to your result, then filtering that position column — which requires a subquery since WHERE runs before window functions.

template: SELECT * FROM (SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS revenue, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(amount) DESC) AS rnk FROM orders GROUP BY 1) t WHERE rnk <= 10 ORDER BY rnk
anti_patterns: Never filter on a window function alias in WHERE
Avoid RANK for deduplication — use ROW_NUMBER which guarantees unique ranks
